agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Memory leak from never-cleaned-up CourierTracker instances on disconnect, plus unguarded JSON.parse that will crash the process on malformed messages."
  },
  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Memory leak: CourierTracker instances never cleaned up on WebSocket close",
      "risk": "Each courier reconnects every ~5 minutes, creating a new CourierTracker. The old instance is never destroyed — no 'close' listener removes the event handlers or nulls the reference. The ws.on('message') closure captures `this`, preventing GC of the CourierTracker even after the socket is dead. Over 3 months this accumulates tens of thousands of orphaned instances per courier.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners()",
      "reproduction": "1. Connect courier, creating CourierTracker #1. 2. Disconnect courier (socket closes). 3. Reconnect courier, creating CourierTracker #2. 4. Repeat. Heap snapshot will show CourierTracker count growing linearly with no collection. After 3 months at 5-min sessions, a single courier produces ~260K orphaned instances.",
      "fix": "Add a 'close' listener in setupListeners that removes all listeners and nulls the ws reference: `this.ws.on('close', () => { this.ws.removeAllListeners(); this.ws = null; });` Additionally, maintain an external Map<string, CourierTracker> and delete the entry on close so no external reference holds the object alive.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service stability — this is the direct cause of the steadily increasing memory usage reported over 3 months."
    },
    {
      "title": "Unguarded JSON.parse crashes process on malformed WebSocket message",
      "risk": "A single malformed message (non-JSON binary frame, partial UTF-8, empty string) throws an unhandled exception inside the 'message' listener. In Node.js, an uncaught exception in a WebSocket event handler will crash the process unless there is a global uncaughtException handler, killing tracking for ALL couriers.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — ws.on('message') callback",
      "reproduction": "Send a non-JSON string (e.g. raw bytes or empty string) over the WebSocket. JSON.parse throws SyntaxError. No try/catch exists. Process crashes or the error bubbles up unhandled.",
      "fix": "Wrap in try/catch: `try { const location = JSON.parse(data); if (typeof location.lat !== 'number' || typeof location.lng !== 'number') return; this.updateLocation(location); } catch (e) { console.warn('Invalid message from courier', this.courierId, e); }`",
      "time_to_fix": "10 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service availability — one bad message from one courier should not take down the entire service."
    },
    {
      "title": "Fire-and-forget DB write with no error handling or backpressure",
      "risk": "updateLocation calls db.couriers.updateLocation without awaiting the result or catching errors. If the DB is slow or down: (1) errors are silently swallowed, (2) unbounded concurrent writes queue up in memory (compounding the leak), and (3) there is no way to detect data loss. Under high courier volume this can exhaust DB connection pools.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()",
      "reproduction": "Simulate DB latency of 500ms with 100 couriers sending updates every second. The unawaited promises accumulate, and if the DB rejects connections, no error is logged.",
      "fix": "Make updateLocation async, await the DB call inside a try/catch, and add basic throttling or debouncing (e.g., only persist the latest location per courier at most once per N seconds) to limit write volume.",
      "time_to_fix": "45 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Data integrity and DB stability under load."
    }
  ],
  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No input validation on lat/lng coordinates",
      "description": "location.lat and location.lng are used directly without validating they are numbers within valid ranges (-90..90, -180..180). NaN, Infinity, or out-of-range values would be written to DB.",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()"
    },
    {
      "title": "console.error in WebSocket error handler is insufficient for production observability",
      "description": "The error handler logs to console but doesn't emit metrics, alert, or attempt reconnection logic. In production, socket errors need structured logging with correlation IDs for debugging.",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — ws.on('error')"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Add rate limiting or debouncing on location updates — GPS sends far more frequently than you need to persist, and throttling reduces DB pressure significantly.",
    "Consider using a Map<courierId, CourierTracker> registry so you can enforce one-tracker-per-courier and explicitly destroy the old one on reconnect.",
    "Add a heartbeat/ping-pong mechanism to detect zombie connections that the 'close' event might miss."
  ],
  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "The memory leak is actively degrading the running service. A fix deploy will reset memory usage but the underlying issue will recur without the close handler fix.",
    "rollback_capability": "Safe to roll back — no schema changes or external dependencies.",
    "backward_compatibility": "No breaking changes; fix is purely additive (new event listener, try/catch).",
    "monitoring_coverage": "None visible — no metrics on active tracker count, DB write failures, or parse errors. Add process.memoryUsage() metric and tracker count gauge at minimum.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": []
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert commit and restart service. Memory leak resumes but no data loss."
  },
  "escalations": [],
  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Memory leak diagnosis — classic missing 'close' handler pattern, directly explains the 3-month memory growth",
      "JSON.parse crash — deterministic, trivially reproducible",
      "Fire-and-forget DB write risk"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether an external registry holds CourierTracker references (would worsen the leak further but code not shown)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "db.couriers.updateLocation returns a Promise (standard for Node.js DB drivers)",
      "No global uncaughtException handler exists to catch the JSON.parse crash",
      "The caller creating new CourierTracker instances does not explicitly destroy old ones on reconnect"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this code for critical issues.

Key observations:
1. Memory leak: No cleanup of WebSocket listeners or references when connection closes
2. No JSON.parse error handling - malformed messages crash the handler
3. No input validation on lat/lng
4. No close handler - when courier disconnects, the CourierTracker instance and its listeners persist
5. db.updateLocation has no error handling and isn't awaited

The memory leak is the headline - couriers disconnect every 5 minutes, new CourierTracker created each time, old ones never cleaned up because there's no 'close' handler and no way to remove listeners/references.
